coredata.dc:title: Withered magnolia-derived BCDs onto 3D flower-like Bi2WO6 for efficient photocatalytic TC degradation and CO2 reduction
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xin
Yu, Yang
Wang, Yuxin
Di, Ying
Liu, Jianqi
Li, Dongyi
Wang, Yemei
Zhu, Zhi
Liu, Huilian
Wei, Maobin
coredata.dc:description: The biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (BCDs, explored from the withered magnolia blossom) modified Bi2WO6 (Bi2WO6/BCDs) was prepared and used to remove the water contaminants. The biomass-derived carbon quantum dots-modified Bi2WO6 was synthesized by the unique hydrothermal technology and dialyzed method, showing excellent photocatalytic degradation activity of tetracycline and CO2 photoreduction performance. The Bi2WO6/BCDs showed a better tetracycline (TC) degradation efficiency than that of Bi2WO6 (49 %) under the visible light, when mass of BCDs was 15 μL. Additionally, photo-electrochemical measurements proved the separation efficiency of electron-holes of Bi2WO6 was enhanced, due to the great electron transmission ability and light absorption of BCDs. The mechanism experiment showed that ·O2 - was the main activity specie in the TC photodegradation process. Meanwhile, the Bi2WO6/BCDs also showed higher CO2 photoreduction performance to CO (18.2 μmol/g) under UV–vis light than the pure Bi2WO6. The CO2 reduction mechanism also investigated by in-situ FTIR. The favorable photocatalytic performances make the waste biomass carbon act as a new resource of application to wastewater treatment and carbon recycling process.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photocatalytic degradation
CO2 photoreduction
Conductive composite photocatalyst
Biomass-derived carbon quantum dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Electron spin resonance spectra and trapping experiments
The TC degradation intermediates and pathways

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples.
SEM images of (a) Bi2WO6, (b) Bi2WO6/BQDs, TEM images of Bi2WO6 (c) and Bi2WO6/BQDs (d), HRTEM images (e) and SAED (f) of Bi2WO6/BQDs.
(a) UV–vis DRS of as-prepared Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs; (b) the bandgap determined from (αhν)2 versus (hν) plots for Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs.
XPS spectra of Bi2WO6, Bi2WO6/BCDs: survey spectrum(a), Bi 4f (b), W 4f (c), O 1s (d) and C 1s (e).
Mott-Schottky curves (a) and XPS valence band spectra (b) of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs.
PL spectra of the Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs (a), N2 adsorption-desorption isothermal and the corresponding SBET, pore size distribution (inset table) of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs (b).
Photocurrent response (a), EIS and magnified image inter (b) of BCDs, Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs.
ESR spectra of Bi2WO6/BCDs: DMPO- . O2 - (a), DMPO- . OH (b) in dark and under visible light irradiation trapping experiment of active species (c).
m/z of TC over Bi2WO6/BCDs degradation: initial solution (a), degradation in 15 min (b), degradation in 30 min (c), degradation in 90 min (d).
Possible degradation intermediate products of TC over Bi2WO6/BCDs.

Adsorption curves of TC on BQDs, Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BQDs (a), photocatalytic degradation of TC solution using different photocatalysts (b), cycling on degradation TC (c), and XRD, SEM and TEM results after five consecutive cycles of Bi2WO6/BQDs (d, e, f).
Photoreduction CO2 productions plots of CO (a), CH4 (b) over different samples. In-situ FT-IR spectra at different time intervals during CO2 reduction upon light illumination (c).
Possible carrier transfer pathways of degradation of TC (a), and CO2 reduction (b) under the Visible or UV–vis light irradiation over Bi2WO6/BCDs.
Schematic illustration of the preparation of Bi2WO6/BCDs.

head.ce:title: Withered magnolia-derived BCDs onto 3D flower-like Bi2WO6 for efficient photocatalytic TC degradation and CO2 reduction

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (BCDs, explored from the withered magnolia blossom) modified Bi2WO6 (Bi2WO6/BCDs) was prepared and used to remove the water contaminants. The biomass-derived carbon quantum dots-modified Bi2WO6 was synthesized by the unique hydrothermal technology and dialyzed method, showing excellent photocatalytic degradation activity of tetracycline and CO2 photoreduction performance. The Bi2WO6/BCDs showed a better tetracycline (TC) degradation efficiency than that of Bi2WO6 (49 %) under the visible light, when mass of BCDs was 15 μL. Additionally, photo-electrochemical measurements proved the separation efficiency of electron-holes of Bi2WO6 was enhanced, due to the great electron transmission ability and light absorption of BCDs. The mechanism experiment showed that ·O2 - was the main activity specie in the TC photodegradation process. Meanwhile, the Bi2WO6/BCDs also showed higher CO2 photoreduction performance to CO (18.2 μmol/g) under UV–vis light than the pure Bi2WO6. The CO2 reduction mechanism also investigated by in-situ FTIR. The favorable photocatalytic performances make the waste biomass carbon act as a new resource of application to wastewater treatment and carbon recycling process.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of economy and society, environmental pollution and CO2 discharge has become more and more serious [1], which have restricted the sustainable development of modern human society, and posed a serious threat to ecosystems and human health. There are many kinds of toxic organic pollutants in contaminated water, including the industrial, animal husbandry [2]. In particular, tetracycline (TC) as one of the most widely used antibiotic in the world for treatment of different diseases in animals and humans. However, the misused or abused of TC will be a serious threat to the health of people and animals [3]. Residual TC in water can cause serious water pollution problems. Accordingly, it is an important task to seek a suitable method to solve above problems. Similarly, reduction of CO2 to high value-added hydrocarbon has been widely concerned, because it helps alleviate the energy shortage and mitigate greenhouse effect.

Photocatalytic technology is considered to be the most promising and green technology to solve the above environmental crisis [4,5]. In recent years, the development of highly-efficient photocatalytic materials has drawn wide attention [6]. Bismuth-based photocatalysts are considered as good candidates due to the interaction between the 2p O and 6 s Bi orbitals at the valence band tops and its potential for visible light response, just like BiVO4 [7], Bi2O3 [8], BiPO4 [9], etc. As the simplest Aurivillius-type oxides, Bi2WO6 has a response in visible light and it has been attracted much increasing attention in virtue of its excellent performance, such as high activity, good chemical stability, its low cost, nontoxicity and high photoactivity due to its special layered structure [10]. However, the unsatisfactory visible light absorption and fast photo-generated carriers’ recombination ineffective charge separation of the original Bi2WO6 have limited its application. Many strategies, just like doping metal or non-metal ions [11,12], coupling with other photocatalysts have been developed to increase the photocatalytic activity of the pure Bi2WO6 [13,14]. The increasing amount of carbon materials have stepped into researchers’ attention for enhancing the photocatalytic ability of the catalysts, such as graphene [15], carbon nanofibers [16], carbon nanotubes [17], carbon quantum dots [18], etc. Among these known carbon materials, biomass carbon as a novel and eco-friendly carbon material, has attracted most recent notice in photocatalytic field.

Lately, biocarbon material has attracted worldwide attention due to its low cost, abundant raw materials, reproducibility, and so on. Moreover, the precursor of biomass carbon is derived from nature's recyclable renewable carbon carriers, and using this kind of biomass as raw material to produce not only saves costs, but also alleviates environmental pollution problems caused by burning a large amount of discarded biomass, which in line with the current trend of the sustainable development of resource. Therefore, the biocarbon modified semiconductor has received extensive attention and made certain research progress in the field of photocatalysis, such as TiO2/CuO, CdSe, g-C3N4 [19–21]. Despite progress of biocarbon, it is still highly pressing to develop a new way to get biocarbon based Bi2WO6 with more efficient activity on TC degradation and CO2 photoreduction.

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), has strong light harvestability, good electronic conductivity, multiple exciton generation, as well as fabulous bulk-to surface transferability electron, have been testified to be as excellent additives on improving photocatalytic activity of pristine semiconductor, such as CDs/Zn2SnO4 [22], CDs/Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 [23], CDs/TiO2 [24], which all showed improved activity. Therefore, hypothetical biomass-derived carbon quantum dots (BCDs) were synthesized from renewable biomasses, which seems to is a green and effective approach to enhance the photocatalytic Bi2WO6 activity. Thus, the application of the BCDs in photocatalytic field could effectively promote the separation of photo-generated electron-hole pairs effectively, thus degrading the water pollutants and photo-reducing CO2 into the high-value chemicals.

Inspired by the above facts, we developed a novel composite photocatalyst of hierarchical flowers globose Bi2WO6/BCDs composites. The photocatalytic performance, adsorption capacity towards TC, electrons transfer performance, and photogenerated carrier lifetime with different proportions of Bi2WO6 and BCDs on the effect of removing TC activity were investigated in detailed. We hope this work could offer a ‘changing waste into wealth’ idea to improve photocatalytic degradation activity of different kind pristine semiconductors with biomass-derived carbon quantum dots and get broader photocatalytic applications.

The Na2WO4·2H2O (AR), Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (AR), H2SO4 (98 %), NaOH (AR), tetracycline (TC), isopropanol (IPA, AR), triethanolamine (TEOA, AR), ascorbic-acid (AA, AR), acetic acid (AR), ethanol (C2H5OH, AR) and 5, 5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co Ltd. Deionized water was used throughout all experiments.

The hierarchical Bi2WO6 samples was synthesized through a template-free hydrothermal method. In a typical process, 0.97 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O were dissolve in 30 mL acetic acid, termed as solution A, which was magnetically stirred for 20 min. Then, 0.33 g of Na2WO4·2H2O were dissolved in 50 mL deionized water, termed solution B. which was magnetically stirred for 20 min. Solution B was added into solution A and magnetically stirred for another 20 min before transferring into a 100 mL Teflon lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated at 180 °C for 25 h. The 3D hierarchical Bi2WO6 are mainly formed through hydrothermal self-assembly. After the autoclave was naturally cooled down to room temperature, the precipitates were filtration and washed by deionized water and ethanol. The precipitate was dried at 80 °C for 8 h for further used.

Picking up the withered magnolia, and the obtained petals were thoroughly washed and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. Then, the cleared petals were fully smashed for further used; Next, the withered magnolia powder was dispersed into the deionized water and ethanol, and magnetic stirred for about 20 min, and the pH value of the precursor solutions was adjusted to pH = 7 by 0.1 M H2SO4 and then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steels autoclave. The mixture was heated to 180 °C and kept for 5 h. After cooling down to the room temperature, the products were dialyzed for 24 h, and the obtained solution was dried in a freeze dryer for 48 h to obtain BCDs, this method is similar to our previous report [25].

The Bi2WO6/BCDs catalysts were synthesized by hydrothermal method, the prepared progress of the binary composites was similar to the pure Bi2WO6 except adding the additional different volumes (5, 10, 15, 20, 25 μL) of BQDs into the Bi2WO6 precursor solution. The prepared photocatalysts were named as Bi2WO6/BCDs-5, Bi2WO6/BCDs-10, Bi2WO6/BCDs-15, Bi2WO6/BCDs-20, and Bi2WO6/BCDs-25. The specific synthesized processes of all samples are illustrated in Scheme 1.

The crystal structure property of the prepared samples was evaluated by the X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns on a D/max-RAX-ray diffractometer (Rigaku, Japan) using Cu Ka radiation source. FT-IR spectra were measured from KBr pellets as the sample matrix on a Nexus 870 FT-IR spectrometer. The transmission electron microscope (TEM) was performed by JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Japan). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was characterized by a Hitachi S-4800 with 5.0 kV scanning voltages (Japan). The nitrogen adsorption-desorption and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) was obtained on a surface area analyzer (NOVA 2200e, Quantachrome). The Ultraviolet visible diffused reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) were carried out via UV–vis spectrophotometer (Cary 300, USA). Photoluminescence (PL) was measured on the Shimadzu RF-5301 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

The photocatalytic activities were evaluated through decomposing TC under visible light irradiation (300 W Xe lamp with a 420 nm cut off filter) and the CO2 photoreduction under the irradiation of UV-Vis light (300 W Xe lamp). 0.05 g catalyst powders were dispersed in aqueous solution of TC (100 mL, 20 mg L−1). Before the photo-degradation the suspensions by ultrasonication and oscillation and after that the suspensions were magnetically stirred in dark for 60 min to get absorption-desorption equilibrium between the photocatalyst and TC. During the illumination, 3 mL reaction solution was centrifuged and measured by UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550). The liquid phase CO2 reduction was carried out in a homemade quartz reactor, 300 W Xe lamp (full spectrum) was used as light source. Firstly, 0.05 g photocatalyst and 90 mL H2O was dispersed into reactor with magnetic stirring, 10 mL TEOA was used as sacrificial agent, kept the CO2 reduction reaction around 25 °C. After pumped gas mixture of CO2 (99.999 %), turn on light. 50 μL gas was sampled at 2.0 h interval and analyzed by gas chromatograph (GC-7920), equipped with FID and N2 as carrier gas.

The photocurrent, electrochemical impedance measurements were carried out on a workstation in a three-electrode model. The Bi2WO6/BCDs was used as the working electrode. The Pt electrode was used as the counter electrode. An Ag/AgCl electrode in saturated KCl solution was employed as the reference electrode. 0.05 g photocatalysts was dispersed in a certain amount ethylene glycol with ultrasonication and oscillation, then coated on glass electrode (1 * 1 cm2).

Fig. 1 presents the typical XRD patterns of the as-prepared composite photocatalysts. For the XRD pattern of BQDs, it could be seen that a conspicuous peak located at the range of 20–30◦, which is the characteristic peak of carbon material, suggesting the BQDs was successfully prepared. The XRD patterns of Bi2WO6 are in good agreement with the reported data, the peaks allocated to (113), (200), (026), (313), (226), (400), (333), and (046) are typical orthorhombic structures of Bi2WO6 (PDF #73-2020) [26], no peaks of other impurities are observed. The stronger and sharp diffraction peaks indicated the great crystalline of Bi2WO6. In addition, for the Bi2WO6/BCDs addition of different content BQDs display accordant diffraction peaks as Bi2WO6, and the peak at around 26° was characteristic peak BQDs. What’s more, the peaks intensities corresponding Bi2WO6 has no obvious change or shifts along with increase of BCDs mass ratio. On the contrary, the characteristic peaks of BCDs in Bi2WO6/BCDs-5 and Bi2WO6/BCD-10 could hardly be seen (around 26°). This is attributed to the BQDs content is too low compared to Bi2WO6 and could not change the electronic structure.

Form the SEM images in Fig. 2a, it could be seen the Bi2WO6 has a 3D hierarchical sphere structure with a diameter about 2.5 µm. After introduction of BCDs (Fig. 2b), the Bi2WO6/BCDs still keeps flower-like multi-stage microspheres structure with uniform size, which composed of numerous nanosheets, meaning the 3D flower-like Bi2WO6 microsphere structure is form by 2D nanosheets overlap together. For further detailed observation the surface of Bi2WO6, we ultrasound Bi2WO6 and obtained the fragments, and TEM image of the Bi2WO6 fragments are displayed in Fig. 2c. It shows that the surface of Bi2WO6 nanosheets is smooth, as well as Bi2WO6/BCDs. The difference is the BCDs (as seen by the tiny, and black dots) highly-interspersed on Bi2WO6 nanosheets without any aggregation, which showed in Fig. 2d. Furthermore, the HRTEM of Fig. 2e reveals that the different lattice fringe belongs to Bi2WO6, and the black spots should be BCDs. The lattice spacings of Bi2WO6 ([200] = [002] = 0.273 nm, [113] = 0.221 nm, [202] = 0.192 nm) can be identified. The corresponding Fast Fourier-Transform (FFT) diffraction pattern Fig. 2(f) exhibited clear diffraction spots with good alignment, confirming the single crystal nature of the hierarchical structure [27]. All the above observations proved that the BCDs are successfully incorporated into Bi2WO6, and the loaded BCDs could conduction electron for improving the photocatalytic activity.

The optical absorbance properties of the Bi2WO6 and the Bi2WO6/BCDs were measured by UV-Vis DRS, and showed in Fig. 3a. The pure Bi2WO6 shows an obviously absorption in the visible light region and the absorption edge located at around 450 nm, which is beneficial to the direct use of solar energy. The sharp decline in the UV-Vis DRS curve shows that the absorption of visible light, which is due to the indirect bandgap transition of the Bi2WO6. Importantly, after the loading of BCDs process, the absorption range has an obviously blue-shift, which may be caused by quantum effect of BCDs. Meanwhile, the light absorption was enhanced compared with Bi2WO6. Therefore, the Bi2WO6/BCDs composite presents more efficient light harvest and utilization ability, which is able to produce more photo-generated charge carriers, resulting higher photocatalytic activity [28]. The band gap energy of all prepared catalysts was determined by the equation: hν = A (hν - E
g ) n/2, where A, h, ν, E
g , and A is the absorption coefficient, the Planck constant, the light frequency, the band gap, and a constant, respectively. Among them, n depends on the optical transition type of the semiconductor. Hence, as a direct transition semiconductor, the n value for the Bi2WO6 is = 1 [29]. By transformation UV–vis DRS result, the bandgap of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs is estimated to be 2.82 eV and 2.86 eV, meaning that bandgap of Bi2WO6 does not move, just enhanced visible-light absorption after loaded BCDs.

The chemical states of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs have been investigated by XPS. Fig. 4a shows the survey spectra off all samples. All signals of C, Bi, W, O elements are detected in the Bi2WO6/BCDs composite, which indicates that Bi2WO6/BCDs is mainly composed by the Bi, O, W and C elements. Fig. 4b shows the binding energy peaks of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2 in Bi2WO6/C, which located at about 164.3 eV and 161 eV, respectively. There results imply that Bi3+ exists in the crystal structure. Compared with pure Bi2WO6, the Bi 4f peak in Bi2WO6/BCDs displays a slight shift about 0.2 eV toward higher binding energy due to the interaction between BCDs and Bi2WO6. The peaks of W 4f at the binding energy of 37.3 eV and 35.1 eV could attribute to the W 4f5/2 and W 4f7/2 (Fig. 4c), respectively. This result proves that the W atoms exists in + 6 oxidation state in the composite [30]. The binding energy peak appears at about 529.4 eV can be attribute to the existent of O 1s (Fig. 4d). The binding energy of O 1s and W 4f also displays more or less higher energy shift. Only one peak at 284.6 eV in pure biocarbon, which is related to the adsorptive adventitious hydrocarbon, while for Bi2WO6/BCDs sample, C 1s peak are divided into three peaks. The other two peaks at around 286.1 eV and 288.3 eV are attributed to C-O and CO, respectively [31]. Therefore, the XPS results imply that the introduction of BCDs does not turn the skeleton structure of Bi2WO6.

To further understand the specific band gap structure of the prepared samples Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs, the Mott-Schottky curves and XPS valence band (VB) spectra of them were tested and showed in Fig. 5. As shown in Fig. 5a, we can obtain the Fermi level potential of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs is about + 0.01 and + 0.11 V (vs Ag/AgCl), and we can convert them to be about + 0.21 and + 0.31 V (vs NHE) as the previously researches [4,21]. Fig. 5b shows the XPS VB spectra of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs is about 1.96 eV and 1.88 eV, respectively. So, we can obtain the specific valance band potential (E
VB ) of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs as + 2.13 and + 2.19 V, and the conduction band potential (E
CB ) of them can be calculated as − 0.65 V and − 0.67 V by the formula: E
CB = E
VB - E
g , respectively. It can be seen that the reduction ability of the composite catalyst is improved than the pure Bi2WO6, which is benefit to the CO2 photoreduction reaction.

To determine more detailed information about the effect of biomass carbon on the photocatalytic reaction, the efficiency of charge migration and transfer was measured by the excitation-dependent PL spectra, which is closely related to the photocatalytic performance of the samples [32,33]. As shown in Fig. 6a, the pristine Bi2WO6 presents a strong emission peak at 470 nm, which accords with the reported results [34]. In particular, Bi2WO6/BCDs composite exhibits a significantly lower PL peak than the Bi2WO6 under the same excitation wavelength, which can be attributed to the intimate interfacial contact of biomass carbon and Bi2WO6. And this interfacial contact can greatly prolong the electron-hole pair lifetime, which is beneficial for the photocatalytic reaction. Moreover, the PL intensity results of the Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs are in accord with the tendency of the photocatalytic activity of them, which proves that the photogenerated carriers’ separation performance directly affect the photocatalytic ability of the catalysts. As known that the specific surface areas (SBET) and pore structures play a vital role in the photocatalytic reaction. Thus, N2 adsorption-desorption and pore-size distribution of Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs were detected and the corresponding results are showed in Fig. 6b. According to the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC) classification, the as-prepared samples exhibit IV-type isotherm with H3-type hysteresis loop, the relative pressure (P/P0) range of 0.2–0.8, indicating the samples had mesoporous structure [35–37]. As the SBET results exhibited in Fig. 6b, the SBET of Bi2WO6/BCDs is smaller than that of Bi2WO6, which means that the specific surface area is not the main factor that determines the photocatalytic activity of the samples.

In order to further illustrate the introduction of BCDs not only improve the light utilization of Bi2WO6, but also reduce the electrons and holes recombination rate. The photocurrent-time (I-t) curves of BCDs, Bi2WO6 and Bi2WO6/BCDs are recorded and displayed in Fig. 7a. For the Bi2WO6/BCDs, the increase in photocurrent means the efficient photogenerated charge carriers’ separation at the interface. Notably, the improvement of photocurrent response can be ascribed to the following two reasons: (i) the introduction of BCDs could provide efficient charge separation (ii) Bi2WO6 has a unique layered structure characterized by [Bi2O2] slab interlacing with a perovskite type WO4 2- layer. This may result in the presence of an internal electrostatic field perpendicular to the [Bi2O2] plate in Bi2WO6 interleaved with the class of perovskite WO4 2- layers, thereby enhancing the photoelectrode pairs in the [010] direction [38]. In addition, the EIS of Nyquist plots analysis also performs to examine the interfacial charge transfer behaviors of on the incorporation of BCDs. As shown in Fig. 7b, the Bi2WO6/BCDs exhibits a smaller circular radius, which means that the Bi2WO6/BCDs has a lower electron transfer resistance than that of Bi2WO6, the inter image is magnified EIS spectra. It is attributed to the tight interface connection by introduce the BCDs to promote faster interface charge transfer efficiency and improvement of photocatalytic property [39]. The above results show that the introduction of BCDs is beneficial to the separation and transfer performance of photogenerated carriers in Bi2WO6/BCDs for improving the photocatalytic activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electron spin resonance spectra and trapping experiments
ce:section.ce:para:
To further investigate the main active species and the mechanism accounting during the photodegradation process, the electron spin resonance (ESR) spin-trap was introduced to analyze the presence of·OH,·O2 - radicals [40–43], the Bi2WO6/BCDs in a methanol for DMPO-·O2 - ( Fig. 8a) and in an aqueous dispersion for DMPO-·OH (Fig. 8b). No obvious DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2 - signal can be detected for the test in dark environment, indicating that the·OH and·O2 − could not generated in Bi2WO6/BCDs systems. By comparison, both signal intensities of·O2 - and·OH can be detected under visible light irradiation. Based on the above analysis, the high activity could be mainly attributed to introduction of BCDs, which can increase the charge separation electrons utilization that produce more ·O2 -. As the E
VB is not positive enough to produce·OH in water, so the detected·OH is mainly derived from H+ reaction with·O2 -. In order to clarify the main active species during the TC photodegradation process, radicals trapping experiments are conducted. As shown in Fig. 8c, the AA, IPA and TEOA were used to characterize·O2 -,·OH and h+, respectively [40,41]. According to the results, it is shown that photocatalytic activity of Bi2WO6/C efficiency of TC is inhibited after AA (degradation activity is 34 %), IPA degradation activity is (55 %), and TEOA degradation activity is 43 %) was added, it greatly inhibited the photocatalytic degradation of TC, these results indicated that the·O2 -,·OH and h+ played the principal roles during the photodegradation process [44,45].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The TC degradation intermediates and pathways
ce:section.ce:para: In order to explain and describe the further identification of the TC intermediates during the photodegradation process [46–48], the high-pressure liquid chromatography-cascade mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) experiment was examined to identify the intermediates. As shown in Fig. 9a-d, the protonated TC molecular ions are detected by the peak at m/z = 443. The proposed degradation products and reaction pathways are displayed in Fig. 10. It is obviously that the TC is almost disappeared after degradation 90 min. As a result, the catalytic activity is enhanced and it is sufficiently degraded into small molecules due to the introduction of the C.

Fig. 11a is TC absorption curves over different catalysts, that the reaction system can reach the adsorption equilibrium in 30 min. It is obviously that Bi2WO6 shows the highest adsorption ability, and BCDs is the lowest. The photocatalytic experimental results indicate the improvement of the catalytic ability of Bi2WO6/BCDs may be attributed to the high ability of the photogenerated carriers’ separation performance not the TC adsorption process. The TC photodegradation efficiency of Bi2WO6/BCDs composites with different amounts of BCDs has been investigated. As shown in Fig. 11b, in the absence of photocatalyst, TC could be hardly decomposed, which indicate the photolysis of TC can be ignored in the experiment. All the samples show remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activity compared with the pure Bi2WO6. As the content of BCDs decreased, the photocatalytic activity of Bi2WO6/BCDs reduced gradually. When BCDs is 15 μL, the binary composite shows the highest activity degradation efficiency (74 %). Meaning that the mass ratio of BCDs is greatly influenced the photocatalytic performance. The appropriate content of BCDs not only enhances the absorption of visible light region, but also facilitates the fast separation of photogenerated charges, thereby improving the photocatalytic activity. On the contrary, the overloading of BCDs is easy to reunite, which hinders the light absorption and restricts the photocatalytic activity. The Bi2WO6/BQDs stability and reusability of the composites are important for the practical application and the results are shown in Fig. 11c. After each reaction cycle, the catalyst was used by centrifugation, washed with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol repeatedly from the mixture separation, and then drying the precipitate in an air oven. It is observed that after five cycling experiments, the degradation efficiency of Bi2WO6/BCDs is lower a bit after five successive cycles, as well as the XRD, SEM and TEM has little change. That is due to the loss of the catalyst in the process of washing and centrifugation and the above results together prove that the Bi2WO6/BCDs composite has great stability in the photocatalytic process. The above results prove that the Bi2WO6/BCDs composite has great stability.

The CO2 reduction activity with all prepared samples as the catalysts is shown in Fig. 12a and b, the CO yield is only about 3.58 μmol/g of Bi2WO6, and that is about 18.2 μmol/g of Bi2WO6/BCDs, which means that the rapid recombination of electron-hole pair, limited the CO2 reduction activity. The improved CO yield after the decoration of BCDs demonstrates that BCDs has the advantage on reducing the photogenerated carriers’ recombination rate. In addition, we can also detect less CH4 generation as shown in Fig. 12b, because of CO generation shows more favorable dynamics than CH4 generation, as selective generation CH4 requires 8-electrons, much more difficult than the transfer of 2-electrons for CO generation. Fig. 12c is the in-situ FT-IR spectra of the CO2 photoreduction process with Bi2WO6/BCDs as the catalyst. There are no peaks before the light illumination. After illumination, many intermediates, including COOH* (1671 and 1458 cm−1), HCO3 − (1718 and 1193 cm−1), b-CO3 2− (1546 and 1345 cm−1), m-CO3 2− (1396 cm−1), can be detected, which means the conduction of the CO2 photoreduction reaction. The increase intensity of the above peaks with prolong irradiation time indicates the accumulation of reduction products [49]. More importantly, the key intermediate products (COOH*) for the formation of CO could be detected at around 1460 and 1670 cm−1, as many previous reported [50].

On the basis of above results, the possible mechanism of photo-induced charge transfer route over Bi2WO6/BCDs was proposed and showed in Fig. 12 (a), when Bi2WO6/BCDs is exposed to visible-light, photogenerated electrons (e-) in the VB of Bi2WO6 can be excited to the CB of Bi2WO6 and holes (h+) must leave in the VB of Bi2WO6 (Eq. (1))[51–53]. Based on the results calculated as described in Fig. 6, the E
CB of Bi2WO6 is more negative than E
0 (E
CB = −0.65 V, O2/·O2 - = −0.28 V). Therefore,·O2 - production from electrons on the surface of Bi2WO6 and plays a vital role in the photodegradation process. Firstly, the photo-generated electrons (e-) of Bi2WO6 further transferred to carbon (Eq. (2)) and the electrons on the surface of carbon reacted with O2 to generate active species·O2 - (Eq. (3)) because of the good electrical conductivity and the electron storing capacity of carbon. Secondly, active species·O2 - may also react with H+ to further produce the lowest content of·OH (Eq. (4)) [1,5]. Both·O2 - and·OH all can oxidize TC (Eqs. (5) and (6)) into the small. It is noteworthy that, the h+ from Bi2WO6 can also directly oxidize TC (Eq. (7)). In the photocatalytic process,·O2 - and h+ are the main active species which can react with TC and degrade it into the micro-molecule. The photogenerated charge carriers’ transfer and photodegradation reaction steps are proposed as follows:
(1) Bi2WO6 + hυ → e-(Bi2WO6) + h+(Bi2WO6)
(2) e- (Bi2WO6) → e-(BCDs)
(3) O2 + e- →·O2 - Major
(4) ·O2 - + 2H+ → 2·OH Minor
(5) ·O2 - + TC → small molecules Prod· Major
(6) ·OH + TC → small molecules Prod Weak
(7) h+ + TC → small molecules Prod

For the CO2 photoreduction process, Bi2WO6/BCDs could be excited under the Visible light irradiation and there will be a large number of photogenerated carriers on the VB and CB (Eq. (8)). TEOA acts as the sacrificial agent to inhibit electron-hole recombination on Bi2WO6 (Eq. (9)). Some CO2 will react with H2O to form HCO3 -, the HCO3 2- will further ionized into CO3 2- in this system, and a lot of H+ will be produced at the same time (Eqs. (10)–(11)) [54]. The electrons on the surface of the composite will activate the adsorbed CO2 molecules (CO2 (g) → CO2 * ), and convert them into the adsorption state COOH* intermediate (Eqs. (12)–(13)) [55]. Subsequently, the COOH* can be reduced into CO by obtaining electron and H+ (Eqs. (14)–(15)), because the E
CB of Bi2WO6/BCDs (−0.67 V) is more negative than the potential of CO2/CO (−0.53 V vs NHE). At the same time, part of CO* eventually reduced to CH4 * by the typical hydrogenation reaction of CO* (Eqs. (16)–(18)). Finally, the obtained CH4 * molecules can become the CH4 through the desorption process (Eq. (19)) [56], the possible mechanism for photoreduction CO2 and charges transfer behaviour showed in Fig. 12 (right). The specific CO2 photoreduction process and the mechanism we discussed above are shown in Fig. 13a and 13b.
(8) Bi2WO6/BCDs + hυ → Bi2WO6/BCDs (e- + h+)
(9) TEOA+ h+ → TEOA+
(10) CO2+ H2O → H+ + HCO3 -
(11) HCO3 - → H+ + CO3 2-
(12) CO2 (g) → CO2*
(13) CO2*+ H++ e- → COOH*
(14) COOH*+ H+ + e- → CO*
(15) CO* → CO
(16) CO* + H+ + e- → CHO*
(17) CHO*+ 2H+ + e- → CH3O*
(18) CH3O*+ 3H+ + 3e- → CH4* +H2O
(19) CH4*→ CH4

In summary, the environmentally and sustainable bio-carbon was employed as raw materials to prepare novel hierarchical flowers globose Bi2WO6/BCDs photocatalyst. With strong interaction between the unique 3D lamellar structure of Bi2WO6 and highly dispersed BCDs, the Bi2WO6/BCDs showed excellent TC photodegradation and CO2 photoreduction activity. The optimal Bi2WO6/BCDs exhibited nearly 80 % TC degradation rate within 90 min with great stability, the ·O2 - was the main active specie in the process of degradation under the Visible light irradiation. For photoreduction, the CO and CH4 yield of Bi2WO6/BCDs is about 18.2 μmol/g and 5.5 μmol/g, which was approximately 5 times and 4 times than that of Bi2WO6 under UV–vis light irradiation. The enhanced photocatalytic activity was originated from the existence of BCDs not only offer the transmission and storage medium of electron, but also enhanced light absorption.

Xin Li: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Funding acquisition. Yang Yu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Yuxin Wang: Formal analysis, Visualization. Ying Di: Formal analysis, Validation. Jianqi Liu: Conceptualization, Supervision. Dongyi Li: Conceptualization, Supervision. Yemei Wang: Writing – review & editing. Zhi Zhu: Validation, Formal analysis. Huilian Liu: Writing – review & editing. Maobin Wei: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of the Program for the Development of Science and Technology of Jilin Province (20220402031GH; 20200801032GH and 20190701021GH) and the program for the Science and Technology of Education Department of Jilin Province (Grant No. JJKH20220441KJ, JJKH20210447KJ and JJKH20220442KJ).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Facile fabrication of novel ceria-based nanocomposite (CYO-CSO) via co-precipitation: electrochemical, photocatalytic and antibacterial performances
